# 📍 Locality Research Guide: California, 1848–1865 (Gold Rush Era)

**Standard Place:** California, United States
**Target Period:** 1848–1865 (Gold Rush onset through the Civil War decade)
**Research Focus:** Miners and settlers who migrated overland or by sea

---

## Jurisdictional Context

California became a U.S. state on September 9, **1850**, bypassing territorial status entirely — a direct consequence of the explosive Gold Rush population surge. Gold was discovered at Sutter's Mill in Coloma on January 24, 1848, triggering migration of roughly **300,000 people** from the rest of the United States and abroad. This created a transient, predominantly male population of extraordinarily diverse geographic origins.

**Key implications for research:**
- Civil county infrastructure formed rapidly but unevenly across the 1850s. Record survival varies sharply by county and by how early that county organized.
- The **1850 U.S. federal census** (the first taken in California) was **partially destroyed** — some county returns are missing entirely.
- No statewide civil registration of births, marriages, or deaths existed in the 1850s; recording was a county-level function.
- Because the population was so mobile and male-dominated, many men left no California paper trail beyond a single census or mining claim entry.

*(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "California Genealogy" — Gold Rush Records and Pre-Statehood Records sections; Wikipedia, "California Gold Rush")*

---

## Record Types, Repositories, and Access

### 1. 🗳️ Census Records

| Record | Coverage | Access Level | Repository |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1850 U.S. Federal Census | California statewide (partial — some counties missing) | Indexed + images | Ancestry.com; FamilySearch |
| 1852 California State Census | Statewide | **88% name-indexed + full-text searchable** | FamilySearch (522-image volume, imageGroupNumber 006601001) |
| 1860 U.S. Federal Census | California statewide | Indexed + images | Ancestry.com; FamilySearch |

**Research note:** The **1852 California State Census** is the single most important Gold Rush-era census, precisely because it captures the peak influx population. It is well-indexed on FamilySearch and also full-text searchable. Use it to bridge the gap left by missing 1850 counties. *(Source: FamilySearch wiki_search, volume_search result imageGroupNumber 006601001)*

---

### 2. ⛏️ Mining Claim Records

| Record | Coverage | Access Level | Repository |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mining Claim Registers | San Francisco, 1849–1855 | **Browse-only images** (340 images; 0% indexed, not full-text) | FamilySearch (imageGroupNumber 006601002) |
| County mining claim records | Varies by county recorder | Physical / microfilm only | County recorder's offices (El Dorado, Tuolumne, Calaveras, Nevada, Placer, Amador, and other Gold Country counties) |

**Research note:** Mining claims were registered at the county level with the county recorder. FamilySearch has a browse-only San Francisco set (1849–1855), but the majority of Mother Lode county registers remain **physical-only** at the courthouse or in microfilm at the California State Archives. The FamilySearch wiki ("California Genealogy — Gold Rush Records") specifically names county recorders' offices as the primary repository. Plan for on-site or microfilm research for most mining counties.

---

### 3. 🚢 Arrival & Migration Records

| Record | Coverage | Access Level | Repository |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ship passenger manifests, San Francisco | 1849–1890s | Physical + some digitized indexes | **National Archives at San Francisco (NARA Pacific Region, San Bruno, CA)** |
| Port Arrival Registers, Sacramento | 1849–1858 | **Browse-only images** (298 images; 0% indexed, not full-text) | FamilySearch (imageGroupNumber 006601003) |
| Overland trail diaries and journals | Ad hoc | Manuscript collections | California State Library; Bancroft Library, UC Berkeley |

**Research note:** Arrivals by sea were the most heavily documented path. The FamilySearch wiki explicitly identifies NARA San Bruno as the holding repository for San Francisco passenger manifests. Arrivals by the overland trail left minimal institutional records — manuscript diaries and journals at the **California State Library** and the **Bancroft Library at UC Berkeley** are the main substitute. *(Source: FamilySearch wiki "California Genealogy — Gold Rush Records"; volume_search result imageGroupNumber 006601003)*

---

### 4. 📋 Vital Records (Births, Marriages, Deaths)

| Collection | Date Range | Records | Access Level | FamilySearch URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| California, County Births | 1850–1919 | 789,456 records / 394,728 images | Indexed + images | [Collection 2017276](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2017276) |
| California, County Marriages | 1850–1952 | 567,890 records / 1,135,780 persons / 283,945 images | Indexed + images | [Collection 1452832](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1452832) |

**Coverage gap:** FamilySearch's `collections_search` returned **no statewide death index** for the 1850s. California did not begin statewide vital registration until 1905. For 1850s deaths, the primary alternatives are:
- **Church burial records** (see below)
- **Cemetery records** (Find A Grave; physical cemetery surveys)
- **Newspaper obituaries** (Newspapers.com; California Digital Newspaper Collection)
- **Probate records** (county courthouses)

*(Source: FamilySearch collections_search, 4 collections returned for California 1848–1865)*

---

### 5. 🗺️ Land Records

| Record | Coverage | Access Level | Repository |
|---|---|---|---|
| Federal land patents (public domain grants) | 1850s–onward | Indexed + images (free) | Bureau of Land Management — **GLO Records** (glorecords.blm.gov) |
| County deeds and conveyances | County-level, from date of organization | Physical / microfilm | County recorder's offices |

**Research note:** California is a federal land (public-domain) state, so the original government grants of land to private individuals appear in BLM GLO records — a critical and free index of early settlers. County deed books capture subsequent private transactions and are held at individual county courthouses. Many Gold Rush counties organized between 1850 and 1852; check the county's formation date against your target period.

---

### 6. ⚖️ Probate & Court Records (including Naturalizations)

| Record | Coverage | Access Level | Repository |
|---|---|---|---|
| Probate files (wills, inventories) | By county, from organization date | Physical / microfilm | County courthouse; California State Archives |
| Naturalization records | Scattered across district, state, and county courts pre-1906 | Physical / microfilm (some indexed) | NARA San Bruno; county courthouses; California State Archives |
| Civil and criminal court files | By county | Physical | County courthouses |

**Research note:** Pre-1906 naturalizations were filed in any court of record — federal district courts, state superior courts, or even county courts. There is no consolidated statewide index. NARA San Bruno holds the Northern California federal district court naturalizations; the California State Archives holds some superior court records. Naturalization papers often state the applicant's country of birth and date of arrival — high-value data for Gold Rush immigrants.

---

### 7. ✝️ Church Records

| Record | Date Range | Access Level | Repository |
|---|---|---|---|
| California Mission records (baptisms, marriages, burials) | 1769–1850 | Indexed + images (98,765 records) | FamilySearch [Collection 1804372](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1804372) |
| Protestant / Catholic parish registers (Gold Rush era) | 1849–onward | Physical; sparse digitization | Individual denomination archives; local parish |

**Coverage note:** The Mission records collection (1769–1850) predates the Gold Rush and primarily documents Native Californians and early Hispanic settlers — background context for pre-statehood families, not direct Gold Rush migrants. Gold Rush-era Protestant and Catholic parish registers are **largely physical-only**, held by the individual congregations or diocesan archives. The Archdiocese of San Francisco Archives is a key repository for Catholic records in Northern California.

---

### 8. 🗞️ Newspapers

| Resource | Coverage | Access | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Historical California newspapers | 1846–present | Subscription | Newspapers.com; California Digital Newspaper Collection (cdnc.ucr.edu — **free**) |

The California Digital Newspaper Collection at UC Riverside is a free, searchable digitized archive that includes 1850s mining-region papers such as the *Alta California* (San Francisco) and various Mother Lode county papers. Obituaries, mining notices, public notices (lost persons, legal announcements), and ship arrivals were all regularly published.

---

### 9. 🗳️ Voter Registration — Great Registers

| Collection | Date Range | Records | Access Level | FamilySearch URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| California, Great Registers | **1866–1910** | 1,345,678 records / 672,839 images | Indexed + images | [Collection 1923340](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1923340) |

**Important note on scope:** The Great Registers begin in **1866**, outside the core 1850s target window. However, they are invaluable for tracking Gold Rush migrants who remained in California into the late 1860s and beyond. Each entry typically records name, age, nativity (birthplace), occupation, and physical description — often the only document to state a miner's country of origin.

---

### 10. 🎖️ Military Records

| Resource | Coverage | Access | Repository |
|---|---|---|---|
| Civil War service & pension records | California volunteers, 1861–1865 | Indexed + images (subscription) | Fold3.com; NARA (Washington, D.C.) |
| Mexican-American War (pre-Gold Rush context) | 1846–1848 | Indexed + images | Fold3.com; NARA |

Many Gold Rush migrants enlisted in California volunteer units during the Civil War. Pension files are especially genealogically rich — they often include family statements, birthplace, marriage information, and widow's claims.

---

### 11. ⚰️ Cemetery Records

| Resource | Access | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Find A Grave | Free (findagrave.com) | Volunteer-transcribed; coverage varies by county |
| County cemetery surveys | Physical / PDF | Often held at county genealogical societies or state library |

---

## Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | What They Hold | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **National Archives at San Francisco (NARA Pacific Region), San Bruno, CA** | Ship passenger manifests, federal court naturalizations, federal land records | In-person; some digitized via Ancestry/FamilySearch |
| **California State Library, Sacramento** | State census, overland trail manuscripts, state archives finding aids, newspapers | In-person; partial digitization |
| **Bancroft Library, UC Berkeley** | Gold Rush manuscript collections, diaries, journals, photographs | In-person (appointment); some digitized |
| **California State Archives, Sacramento** | County court records on microfilm, state agency records | In-person; microfilm loan |
| **County Courthouses / Recorder's Offices** | Deeds, mining claims, probate files, marriages, births | In-person; some records on FamilySearch microfilm |
| **Bureau of Land Management — GLO Records** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents | Free online search |
| **California Digital Newspaper Collection** (cdnc.ucr.edu) | 1850s California newspapers | Free online full-text search |

---

## Coverage Gaps to Know

| Gap | Explanation |
|---|---|
| 1850 census partial destruction | Some county returns are missing; use 1852 state census as substitute |
| No statewide death registration until 1905 | Rely on probate, church, cemetery, and newspaper records |
| Most mining claim registers are physical-only | County courthouses in the Mother Lode region (El Dorado, Tuolumne, Calaveras, etc.) |
| Pre-1906 naturalizations are scattered | No single index; check NARA, county courts, and state archives separately |
| FamilySearch volume_search returned 14 total volumes but paginated results were limited | Additional digitized volumes likely exist beyond the 3 shown — browse FamilySearch catalog directly for California 1848–1865 |
| FamilySearch wiki place-page sections returned only generic content | Consult the FamilySearch Wiki "California Genealogy" article directly at [familysearch.org/en/wiki/California_Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/California_Genealogy) for fuller guidance |

---

## Suggested Starting Sequence for a Gold Rush Ancestor

1. **1852 California State Census** (FamilySearch, 88% indexed) — most complete Gold Rush-era enumeration
2. **1850 and 1860 federal censuses** — Ancestry or FamilySearch
3. **Ship passenger manifests** — if the ancestor arrived by sea (NARA San Bruno; Ancestry)
4. **BLM GLO land patents** — glorecords.blm.gov — confirms presence and settlement location
5. **County mining claim registers** — county courthouse or California State Archives microfilm
6. **California Digital Newspaper Collection** — free full-text search of 1850s papers
7. **Great Registers (1866+)** — FamilySearch Collection 1923340 — for confirmed California residents after 1866
8. **NARA San Bruno naturalizations** — if immigrant origin is known or suspected

Would you like a targeted research plan built around a specific ancestor or county, or more detail on any of these record types?

# Locality Guide: New York City (1700s Colonial Period)

## Jurisdiction Overview

- **Formed:** New Amsterdam established 1626 as a Dutch trading post; seized by the English in 1664 and renamed New York
- **Administrative center:** City Hall, Manhattan (the City and County of New York)
- **Parent jurisdiction during 1700s:** New York Province, under British colonial rule (1664–1783)
- **Economy:** Major commercial port, maritime trade, merchant activity, artisan crafts
- **Ethnic composition:** Dutch settlers (the original founding community), English colonists, Huguenot refugees, African enslaved and free persons, small numbers of German, Jewish, and Irish settlers
- **Religious denominations:** Dutch Reformed (the oldest continuous congregation), Anglican/Church of England (Trinity Church, founded 1697), Presbyterian, Lutheran, Quaker, Jewish (Congregation Shearith Israel, founded 1654), and small Catholic and Baptist communities
- **Key historical events:** The French and Indian War (1754–1763), British Proclamation of 1763, pre-Revolutionary unrest and occupation (1776–1783)

---

## Boundary Changes — Critical for This Period

This is one of the most important research cautions for colonial New York City:

- **"New York City" in the 1700s meant Manhattan only** — the City and County of New York. The other modern boroughs were entirely separate jurisdictions with their own record-keeping:
  - **Brooklyn** = Kings County
  - **Queens** = Queens County
  - **Staten Island** = Richmond County
  - **The Bronx** = part of Westchester County (until 1874)
- If your ancestor lived in any of those areas, their records were created and filed in those counties, not in the City and County of New York. You must search the relevant county's records separately.
- Within Manhattan, the city was administratively divided into **wards**, and some records (especially tax lists) are organized by ward rather than surname.

---

## Available Record Types

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)
- **Start date:** Civil registration of births and deaths did not begin in New York until **1880** statewide; civil marriages not until **1847**. There is **no civil registration** for the 1700s.
- **Pre-registration alternatives:** Church registers are the **only systematic source** for vital events (baptisms, marriages, burials) in colonial New York City. This makes church record coverage essential — see below.

---

### Church Records ⭐ *Primary vital records source for this period*

Church registers are the closest equivalent to birth, marriage, and death records for the 1700s. Multiple denominations kept continuous registers.

**FamilySearch Collection:** *New York, Church Records, 1640–1940* (Collection ID 1469062) — 2,345,678 records with 1,172,839 images. Date range 1640–1940 covers the full colonial period. Available at [FamilySearch](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1469062). Access: **Free**.

**Denominations and specific records found by tools:**

| Denomination | Record content | Language | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dutch Reformed | Baptisms, marriages, burials (continuous from 1639) | **Dutch** through much of the 1700s | Oldest continuous series in NYC |
| Anglican / Church of England | Baptisms, marriages, burials | English | Trinity Church (founded 1697) is the key parish |
| Presbyterian | Baptisms, marriages, burials | English | |
| Lutheran | Baptisms, marriages | German or Dutch | |
| Quaker | Births, marriages, deaths (meeting minutes) | English | Society of Friends records |
| Jewish | Circumcisions, marriages, burials | Hebrew/English | Congregation Shearith Israel (Sephardic) |

**Digitization level for Dutch Reformed registers (1700–1776):** FamilySearch volume search returned **one digitized volume** (image group 007733180) covering NYC Dutch Reformed Church Registers, 1700–1776 — 211 images, language Dutch, **0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable**. This means these registers are available online but must be **browsed image by image**. No name search is possible; you must scroll through pages.

**Physical repositories for original church records:**
- Reformed Church in America Archives (New Brunswick Theological Seminary, New Jersey) — Dutch Reformed registers
- Trinity Church Archives (Manhattan) — Anglican/Episcopal registers
- Presbyterian Historical Society (Philadelphia) — Presbyterian records
- New-York Historical Society — manuscript church collections
- New York Genealogical & Biographical Society — transcriptions and indexes

**Language note:** Dutch Reformed records well into the 1700s were often recorded in Dutch. Factor in translation needs when working with these registers.

---

### Census Records

- **No colonial-era census** survives for New York City in any systematic form comparable to later enumerations.
- **First federal census (1790)** captures only the very end of this period, after the Revolution. The 1790 census lists heads of household by name, with tallies of free persons and enslaved persons — not a full nominal list of all residents. It is available indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry.
- **Substitute enumerations:** Colonial-era **tax assessment lists** organized by ward are the best proxy for a census (see Tax Records below). The wiki search confirms that tax assessment lists survive for some years and wards and list residents by name.

---

### Probate Records ⭐ *Well-documented for this period*

**FamilySearch Collection:** *New York, Probate Records, 1629–1971* (Collection ID 1542780) — 987,654 records with 493,827 images. Covers the full colonial period. Available at [FamilySearch](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1542780). Access: **Free**.

- **Prerogative Court of New York:** During the colonial period, wills were proved before the Prerogative Court of the Province of New York — a provincial-level (not county-level) court. This means wills from across the colony, including NYC, were proved centrally.
- **Abstracts of Wills:** The New-York Historical Society published a multi-volume *Abstracts of Wills* series covering the Prerogative Court of New York. The wiki search confirms these are indexed — an invaluable finding aid for colonial estates.
- **Record types:** Wills, estate inventories, letters of administration, guardianship files
- **Where held:** New York State Archives (Albany); New-York Historical Society (published abstracts)

---

### Land Records ⭐ *Well-documented for this period*

**FamilySearch Collection:** *New York, Land Records, 1630–1975* (Collection ID 2078654) — 1,567,890 records with 783,945 images. Covers the full colonial period. Available at [FamilySearch](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2078654). Access: **Free**.

- **System:** Metes and bounds (not rectangular survey — New York is a state-land state)
- **Colonial land grants and patents:** Land in colonial New York was granted by the royal governor. The New York Colonial Manuscripts (at the New York State Archives) contain land patents, grants, and related records for this period.
- **Land conveyances:** Deeds recorded for the City and County of New York are held at the New York County Clerk's office (Manhattan) and are among the best-preserved colonial records.
- **Where held:** New York County Clerk (Manhattan Surrogate's Court building); New York State Archives; FamilySearch digitized collection above

---

### Court Records

- **Mayor's Court of New York City:** The primary civil court for colonial Manhattan. The wiki search confirms Mayor's Court records survive and contain names of parties in civil suits — useful for locating residents and establishing identities. Records are held at the **New York City Municipal Archives** and partially at the **New York State Archives**.
- **New York Colonial Manuscripts:** The wiki search specifically names the *New York Colonial Manuscripts at the New York State Archives* as a key source. These are the official provincial government records including court proceedings, council minutes, and administrative documents.
- **Digitization level:** Mayor's Court records and colonial manuscripts are largely **physical only or microfilm** — not in major online databases. Plan for an archival visit or a mail/digitization request.

---

### Military Records

- **Relevant conflicts:** King William's War (1689–1697, overlaps very start of century), Queen Anne's War (1702–1713), King George's War (1744–1748), French and Indian War (1754–1763), American Revolution (1775–1783)
- **Colonial militia rolls:** The wiki search confirms that colonial New York Province maintained militia rolls listing men of military age — useful for identifying male residents by name, even apart from military service
- **Revolutionary War records:** Service records and pension files are at the **National Archives (NARA)**; accessible via Fold3 (subscription). New York State also holds muster rolls and related records at the **New York State Archives**.
- **Digitization level:** Militia rolls are primarily physical/microfilm. Revolutionary War pension files are **indexed + images** at Fold3 and partially at FamilySearch.

---

### Cemetery Records

- **Major colonial burying grounds:**
  - Trinity Churchyard (Manhattan) — active from 1697; one of the oldest continuously used burial grounds in NYC
  - Dutch Reformed Church burying grounds
  - Congregation Shearith Israel's cemetery (Chatham Square, Manhattan) — oldest Jewish cemetery in the US, burials from 1656
- **Online coverage:** Find A Grave is curated in the external links for this place — cemetery records are available at [FindAGrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com/), though colonial-era coverage is incomplete
- **Physical records:** Sexton records and burial registers are held by the individual churches; some have been transcribed and published by the New York Genealogical & Biographical Society

---

### Newspaper Records

- **The New-York Gazette** (founded 1725) — the first newspaper in colonial New York; Wikipedia confirms it published during this period. Contains legal notices, ship arrivals, and occasional vital event notices.
- **The New-York Weekly Journal** (1733–) and other colonial papers followed
- **Content value:** Obituaries (for wealthier residents), marriage announcements, runaway slave and servant notices, legal notices (estate sales, land auctions), ship manifests
- **Where held:** Digitized colonial newspapers are available through **Newspapers.com** (subscription — curated in external links for this place), the **New York State Library**, and the **American Antiquarian Society**. Some issues are freely accessible through **Chronicling America** (Library of Congress), though colonial-era coverage there is limited.

---

### Immigration / Port Records

- **New York was the primary colonial port of entry** for the northeastern seaboard during the 1700s.
- **Passenger lists:** Pre-1820 passenger lists are fragmentary — systematic arrival manifests were not required until 1820. Some colonial-era ship lists survive and have been published; check the **New York Genealogical & Biographical Society** for compiled lists.
- **Naturalization:** Colonial-era naturalizations were granted by the provincial government or by individual colonies. Records are in the New York Colonial Manuscripts and some have been published in genealogical journals.
- **Where held:** NARA (available via [Archives.gov](https://www.archives.gov/) — curated in external links); New York State Archives for colonial-era naturalization acts

---

### Tax Records

- **Colonial ward tax lists:** The FamilySearch wiki search confirms tax assessment lists survive for some years and wards in colonial New York City, organized by ward (not alphabetically). These function as a **substitute census** for years when no enumeration exists.
- **FamilySearch Collection (end of period):** *New York, Tax Assessment Rolls of Real and Personal Estates, 1799–1804* (Collection ID 1678432) — 123,456 records, 61,728 images — just catches the very end of the 1700s (1799). Available at [FamilySearch](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1678432). Access: **Free**.
- **Earlier tax lists:** Pre-1799 colonial tax lists are held at the **New York City Municipal Archives** and **New York State Archives** — largely physical or microfilm.

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | Relevant collections | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | *NY Church Records 1640–1940* (2.3M records); *NY Land Records 1630–1975* (1.6M records); *NY Probate Records 1629–1971* (987K records); *NY Tax Assessment Rolls 1799–1804* (123K records); Dutch Reformed NYC registers 1700–1776 (browse-only, 211 images) | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | Various NY colonial collections; 1790 Federal Census; military records | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Revolutionary War service records, pension files, colonial military records | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical NYC newspapers including colonial-era titles | Subscription |
| **Find A Grave** | Trinity Churchyard and other NYC colonial cemeteries | Free |
| **NARA (Archives.gov)** | Federal records, naturalization, military pension files | Free (some digitized) |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Key colonial holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **New York State Archives** (Albany) | New York Colonial Manuscripts; court records; land patents; provincial government records | In-person; some holdings on microfilm at FHL |
| **New York City Municipal Archives** (Manhattan) | Mayor's Court records; city government records; early tax lists | In-person; some records on microfilm |
| **New-York Historical Society** (Manhattan) | *Abstracts of Wills* series (indexed); manuscript church collections; newspapers | In-person; some finding aids online |
| **New York Genealogical & Biographical Society** (Manhattan) | Extensive colonial research aids; published transcriptions of church and vital records; compiled passenger lists | Members + in-person; some databases online |
| **Trinity Church Archives** (Manhattan) | Anglican baptism, marriage, burial registers from 1697 | Contact in advance; some published |
| **Reformed Church in America Archives** (New Brunswick, NJ) | Dutch Reformed church registers for NYC and region | In-person; microfilm at FHL |
| **New York County Clerk** (Manhattan Surrogate's Court) | Colonial-era land deeds and conveyances | In-person |

---

## Records NOT Online

These significant record classes exist but are not fully available in online databases:

- **Colonial ward tax lists (pre-1799)** — held at NYC Municipal Archives and NY State Archives; physical/microfilm only
- **Mayor's Court of New York City records** — physical at NYC Municipal Archives; not in major online databases
- **New York Colonial Manuscripts** — at NY State Archives; some on microfilm through FHL but not broadly digitized
- **Most pre-1776 church registers** not yet in the FamilySearch indexed collections — the Dutch Reformed NYC volume (1700–1776) is online but browse-only; many others remain physical
- **Colonial naturalization records** — published in part in genealogical journals; originals at NY State Archives
- **Colonial-era passenger lists** — fragmentary, published in journals and books; no comprehensive online database

---

## Known Record Losses

- **No major courthouse fire** is recorded for colonial Manhattan comparable to losses in other jurisdictions, which means record survival is relatively good for this period.
- **Revolutionary War disruption (1776–1783):** The British occupation of New York City lasted the entire war. Some civic and private records were disrupted or lost during this period. Church record-keeping continued more consistently than government records during occupation.
- **Dutch-period records:** Some records from the earliest Dutch colonial period (pre-1664) were lost or damaged in the transition to English rule; however, records generally survived into the British period.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Start with church records — they are your vital records.** There is no civil registration for the 1700s. Dutch Reformed registers (browse-only at FamilySearch, image group 007733180) and Trinity Church Anglican registers are the first places to look for baptisms, marriages, and burials.

2. **Know which "New York City" your ancestor lived in.** Colonial NYC = Manhattan only. If ancestors were in Brooklyn, Queens, Staten Island, or the Bronx, their records are in Kings, Queens, Richmond, or Westchester County respectively — completely separate record sets.

3. **Dutch Reformed records are often in Dutch.** Even decades after English rule began in 1664, many Dutch Reformed registers were kept in Dutch. Plan for translation assistance.

4. **Check the *Abstracts of Wills* series first for probate.** The New-York Historical Society's published abstracts of Prerogative Court wills are indexed and widely available in research libraries — far faster than searching original volumes. The FamilySearch Probate Records collection (1629–1971) is the digital complement.

5. **Use ward tax lists as a census substitute.** For the many decades before 1790 with no census, colonial tax lists by ward are the best population proxy. They are physical/microfilm at the NYC Municipal Archives and NY State Archives.

6. **The New York Genealogical & Biographical Society (NYG&B)** is the single most important specialized repository for this research. Their published *New York Genealogical and Biographical Record* journal spans over 150 years and contains transcribed colonial records, compiled genealogies, and finding aids not available elsewhere.

7. **Consult the FamilySearch Wiki article "New York City Genealogy"** (source for the colonial records and tax/military sections of this guide) for additional depth and links to specific resources.

8. **For the Revolutionary War period (1775–1783),** check both loyalist and patriot records — NYC was a loyalist stronghold during British occupation, and some families were displaced or had records disrupted. Loyalist claims records at the British National Archives can document NYC families who remained loyal and later left.

9. **The 1790 Federal Census** (the earliest surviving federal census) covers the immediate post-colonial period and is a useful anchor point — work backwards from it into church, probate, land, and tax records to extend research into the mid-1700s.

---

# Locality Guide: Virginia — American Revolutionary War Era (1775–1783)

## Jurisdiction overview

- **Status during period:** Virginia transitioned from a British colony to a U.S. state during this exact period — declaring independence in 1776 and ratifying the federal Constitution in 1788. County-level record-keeping had been in place since the 1630s, making Virginia one of the best-documented early American jurisdictions.
- **Administrative center:** Richmond (capital from 1780; previously Williamsburg)
- **Parent jurisdiction:** British Crown (to 1776), then Commonwealth of Virginia
- **Population during period:** No population figures were returned by the population tool for this window. The first federal census (1790) enumerated Virginia at approximately 747,000 — but that census is **destroyed** (see Known Record Losses below). Estimates for the 1775–1783 period place the free population at roughly 400,000–500,000.
- **Economy:** Predominantly tobacco agriculture; also grain, timber, and iron production. Tobacco plantations dominated the Tidewater; subsistence and mixed farming in the Piedmont and western counties.
- **Ethnic composition:** Predominantly English settlers; Scots-Irish and German communities in the Shenandoah Valley and western Piedmont; a substantial enslaved African population across the Tidewater and Piedmont.
- **Religious denominations:** Anglican/Episcopal (established church until Virginia's 1776 constitution disestablished it), Presbyterian (especially Shenandoah Valley), Baptist, Methodist (growing rapidly in the 1780s), Quaker (particularly in the northern Piedmont), Lutheran and German Reformed (Valley of Virginia).
- **Key historical events:** Virginia contributed more soldiers to the Continental Army than any other colony (per the FamilySearch Wiki article *Virginia Military Records*). Major engagements on Virginia soil included the Yorktown campaign (1781). The Anglican Church's disestablishment in 1776 fragmented parish-based record-keeping mid-period.

---

## Boundary changes — critically important for this period

- **Western territory:** During 1775–1783, Virginia's claimed territory extended far to the west, encompassing what is now **Kentucky** (separated as a state in 1792) and most of present **West Virginia** (separated in 1863). Families in those areas appear in Virginia county records during this period — Kentucky County (est. 1776), then Fayette, Jefferson, and Lincoln Counties (est. 1780).
- **County formation:** Numerous new counties were carved from existing ones throughout this period as settlement spread westward. Always identify which county held authority over a specific location at the event date — earlier records may reside in the parent county's courthouse.
- **No boundary changes for the core Tidewater and Piedmont counties** affected record continuity in the east.

**Research implication:** If your ancestor was in western Virginia, search both the original Virginia county records and, if the county later became part of Kentucky or West Virginia, the successor state's archives as well.

---

## Available record types

### Military records ⭐ PRIMARY FOCUS

Virginia's Revolutionary War military records are the richest category for this period and exist across several distinct record groups.

**Continental Line service records and muster rolls**
- Held at **NARA, Record Group 93** (War Department Collection of Revolutionary War Records)
- Content: compiled service records for Virginia soldiers in the Continental Army — enlistment, pay vouchers, muster rolls, strength returns
- **Access:** Digitized and name-indexed on **Fold3** (subscription); many also on **Ancestry.com**; originals at NARA in Washington, D.C.
- Digitization level: **Indexed + images** (via Fold3/Ancestry)

**Virginia militia records**
- Held at the **Library of Virginia, Richmond**
- Content: muster rolls, pay vouchers, officer commissions, and returns for Virginia county militias — separate from the Continental Line
- **Access:** Partially microfilmed; some digitized. The Library of Virginia's online catalog identifies what has been filmed. Physical originals require in-person visit or written request.
- Digitization level: **Microfilm / physical** (varying by county)

**FamilySearch collection — Virginia, Military Records, 1775–1783**
- FamilySearch collection ID 1945678; date range 1775–1783
- Tool returned 345,678 records and 172,839 images
- **Access:** [FamilySearch.org](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1945678) — free
- Note: The collection tool did not return record-searchability percentages, so browse-ability vs. full name-indexing cannot be confirmed from tool data alone — verify at FamilySearch before searching.

**Pension and bounty-land warrant application files**
- Held at **NARA, Record Group 15**
- Content: pension applications filed by veterans (or their widows) after the war, often containing detailed personal narrative — age, birthplace, military service history, family information, and sometimes witness depositions. These are among the **genealogically richest records** of the Revolutionary era.
- **Access:** Digitized and name-indexed on **Fold3** (subscription) and **Ancestry.com** (subscription); free index at **Fold3's free Revolutionary War pension index**; originals at NARA
- Digitization level: **Indexed + images**

**Virginia State Navy records**
- Per the FamilySearch Wiki article *Virginia Military Records*: Virginia's state navy records have been **published** — check the Library of Virginia and major genealogical libraries for the printed volumes.
- Digitization level: **Published volumes** (physical repositories)

**Post-war bounty land — Virginia Military District of Ohio**
- After the Revolution, Virginia granted bounty lands in what became the Virginia Military District of Ohio to its veterans.
- Warrant and survey records connecting veterans to their claims are at the **Virginia State Library (Library of Virginia)** and the **Ohio History Connection** (for survey plats filed in Ohio).
- Per the FamilySearch Wiki article *Virginia Military Records, Post-War Bounty Lands*: these records link veterans to their families and land locations.
- Digitization level: **Microfilm / physical**

**Loyalist records**
- Families with Loyalist (Tory) connections left different records: confiscated property records, oaths of allegiance, and British claims commission files. The latter are held at **The National Archives (UK), Audit Office Records (AO 12, AO 13)**, and have been partly published and microfilmed.
- Digitization level: **Microfilm / physical** for British records; some published transcriptions available

---

### Tax records — critical census substitute

- **Virginia, Tax Records, 1782–1850** — FamilySearch collection ID 1456723; 456,789 records, 228,394 images; [FamilySearch.org](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1456723) — free
- The **1782 personal property tax** is the single most important substitute for the destroyed 1790 Virginia federal census. It lists free white male tithables (generally age 16+), slaves, livestock, and property — and is the earliest near-statewide enumeration surviving from this period.
- Held originally at county courthouses; extensively microfilmed and partially digitized by the Library of Virginia and FamilySearch.
- Tax records for most Virginia counties survive from the early 1780s onward, though some counties have gaps.
- Digitization level: **Indexed + images** (FamilySearch); **physical** for some counties

---

### Land records

- **Virginia, Land, Marriage, and Will Records, 1600–1900** — FamilySearch collection ID 1734285; 2,567,890 records, 1,283,945 images; [FamilySearch.org](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1734285) — free
- Virginia is a **state-land state** (not federal-land): land grants and patents were issued by the colonial and later state government, not the federal General Land Office. Do not search the BLM-GLO website for Virginia patents.
- **Virginia Land Office Patents and Grants** (from 1623): held at the Library of Virginia; digitized and searchable at [lva.virginia.gov](https://lva.virginia.gov)
- **County deed books**: held at county courthouses; microfilmed by the Virginia State Library and FamilySearch; many digitized
- **Wartime land activity**: look for grants to veterans, wartime sales and purchases (men going to war often settled debts or conveyed land), and confiscated Loyalist estates sold off under state authority
- Digitization level: **Indexed + images** (FamilySearch collection); **physical** at county courthouses for originals

---

### Church records

- **Virginia, Church Records, 1680–1929** — FamilySearch collection ID 1598230; 1,234,567 records, 617,283 images; [FamilySearch.org](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1598230) — free
- **Anglican/Episcopal vestry books and parish registers** (pre-1776): the established church kept registers of baptisms, marriages, and burials. Virginia's 1776 constitution disestablished the Anglican Church, ending compulsory parish registration mid-period. Many pre-1776 registers survive at the **Library of Virginia** and have been published or microfilmed.
- **Post-1776 gap**: after disestablishment, vital registration shifted to county marriage bonds/licenses (civil, county level) and individual denominational records. Coverage is uneven.
- **Presbyterian records**: particularly strong in the Shenandoah Valley and western Piedmont; held by individual congregations and the **Presbyterian Historical Society** (Philadelphia and Montreat, NC)
- **Baptist records**: growing rapidly during this period; held by individual churches; partially microfilmed
- **Quaker records**: monthly meeting minutes recording births, marriages, and deaths; held at **Haverford College Quaker Collection** and **Friends Historical Library** (Swarthmore), with Virginia meetings represented
- Digitization level: **Indexed + images** (FamilySearch collection); **physical** at denomination archives and congregations

---

### County court records

- **Virginia, County Court Records, 1650–1920** — FamilySearch collection ID 2134567; 1,789,012 records, 894,506 images; [FamilySearch.org](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2134567) — free
- County court order books are among the most important wartime records. They document: militia officer appointments and musters, estates of soldiers killed in service, apprenticeships of orphans (children of deceased soldiers), road and bridge overseer appointments, and local governance decisions.
- Originals at county courthouses; many microfilmed by the Library of Virginia and FamilySearch
- Digitization level: **Indexed + images** (FamilySearch collection); **physical** at county courthouses

---

### Probate records (wills, inventories, administrations)

- Part of the **Virginia, Land, Marriage, and Will Records, 1600–1900** FamilySearch collection (ID 1734285)
- Wills and estate inventories of soldiers who died during or after the war are particularly valuable — they name heirs (spouse, children, siblings), list property, and often name neighbors as witnesses or appraisers.
- Guardianship records document minor children left by deceased soldiers.
- Held at county courthouses; microfilmed and partly digitized
- Digitization level: **Indexed + images** (FamilySearch); **physical** at county courthouses

---

### Vital records (civil registration)

- **Virginia did not have statewide civil birth and death registration during 1775–1783.** Statewide registration began in 1853 and was poorly enforced until 1912. No civil birth or death records exist at the state level for the Revolutionary era.
- **Marriage records**: county-level marriage bonds and licenses survive for most Virginia counties from the late 18th century onward; part of the FamilySearch "Land, Marriage, and Will Records" collection and the Library of Virginia's holdings.
- **Pre-1853 vital events** must be reconstructed from church registers, family bibles, estate inventories, and tax records.
- Digitization level: Marriage bonds/licenses — **indexed + images** (FamilySearch); births/deaths — **no civil records exist for this period**

---

### Cemetery records

- **Find A Grave** ([findagrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com)) — curated external link returned for Virginia; free. Covers thousands of Virginia cemeteries including 18th-century church graveyards.
- Many Revolutionary-era gravestones in Virginia churchyards have been transcribed by the **Daughters of the American Revolution (DAR)** and local genealogical societies — check the DAR's Genealogical Records Committee (GRC) reports.
- The **Association for the Preservation of Virginia Antiquities** and local historical societies hold cemetery surveys.
- Digitization level: **Indexed** (Find A Grave, for transcribed entries); **physical** for uninscribed or transcription-only surveys

---

### Newspapers

- Virginia had several newspapers publishing during 1775–1783, including the *Virginia Gazette* (Williamsburg, multiple competing editions) and the *Virginia Journal* (Alexandria). These carried lists of militia officers, notices of deserters, bounty announcements, estates of deceased soldiers, and wartime proclamations.
- **Newspapers.com** — curated external link returned; subscription. Covers some Virginia titles.
- The **Library of Virginia** and **College of William & Mary** hold microfilm of 18th-century Virginia newspapers.
- The *Virginia Gazette* has been digitized by the Colonial Williamsburg Foundation and is partially searchable online.
- Digitization level: **Browse-only images** (microfilm) for most; some issues **indexed** via Newspapers.com or specific digital projects

---

### Census records

- **1790 federal census for Virginia — DESTROYED.** The British burned Washington in 1814, destroying the Virginia 1790 schedules. **No federal census exists for Virginia for 1790.**
- The earliest surviving federal census for Virginia is **1810** (with partial 1800 survival).
- **Substitute:** The 1782 and 1783 personal property tax lists function as the de facto census for the Revolutionary era — use FamilySearch collection ID 1456723.
- Digitization level: 1782/1783 tax lists — **indexed + images** (FamilySearch); 1790 census — **destroyed**

---

### DAR and lineage society records

- The **Daughters of the American Revolution** maintains an extensive index of proven Revolutionary War patriots and their lineage documentation. The DAR Patriot Index is searchable at [dar.org](https://www.dar.org).
- DAR application files often contain compiled genealogical research on Virginia Revolutionary families — held at DAR headquarters (Washington, D.C.) and available on microfilm at many libraries.
- The **Sons of the American Revolution (SAR)** maintains a parallel database.
- These are secondary sources but can point to primary records already located by other researchers.
- Digitization level: **Online index** (DAR/SAR websites); applications — **physical** at DAR HQ or microfilm

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | What's available for Virginia, 1775–1783 | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | 5 collections covering church records, land/marriage/wills, military records 1775–1783, tax records, county court records | Free |
| **Fold3** | Continental Line compiled service records (NARA RG 93); pension files (NARA RG 15); name-indexed with images | Subscription |
| **Ancestry.com** | Federal census (note: 1790 destroyed); military pension files; land records; newspaper collections | Subscription |
| **Find A Grave** | Cemetery transcriptions statewide | Free |
| **Newspapers.com** | Select Virginia newspaper titles | Subscription |
| **Library of Virginia digital portal** ([lva.virginia.gov](https://lva.virginia.gov)) | Land Office patents/grants; some county records; digital newspaper archive | Free |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access method |
|---|---|---|
| **National Archives (NARA), Washington, D.C.** | RG 93 (Continental Army service records, muster rolls); RG 15 (pension and bounty-land warrant files) | In-person or order copies; most digitized on Fold3/Ancestry |
| **Library of Virginia, Richmond** | Virginia militia records; Land Office patents; county court and deed microfilm; state records; newspaper collection | In-person; microfilm loan through interlibrary loan; online catalog |
| **Virginia Museum of History and Culture (formerly Virginia Historical Society), Richmond** | Manuscript collections, family papers, DAR GRC reports | In-person |
| **County courthouses (circuit court clerk)** | Original deed books, will books, court order books, marriage bonds | In-person or written request; access varies by county |
| **The National Archives (UK), Kew** | Loyalist claims records (AO 12, AO 13); British Army muster rolls | In-person or online via Ancestry/FindMyPast |
| **Presbyterian Historical Society, Philadelphia** | Virginia Presbyterian congregation records | In-person or written request |
| **Haverford / Swarthmore Quaker collections** | Virginia Quaker meeting minutes (births, marriages, deaths, disownments) | In-person; some microfilm |
| **Ohio History Connection, Columbus** | Virginia Military District of Ohio survey plats and warrant records | In-person; some digitized |

---

## Records NOT online

- **Virginia county militia records** (Library of Virginia) — partially microfilmed but not comprehensively digitized or name-indexed; many require in-person research or written requests to the Library of Virginia
- **Virginia State Navy published volumes** — in physical libraries only
- **Virginia Military District of Ohio warrant/survey files** — in physical repositories (Library of Virginia; Ohio History Connection)
- **Loyalist claims records** (British National Archives, AO 12/AO 13) — microfilmed but not fully name-indexed online
- **Original Anglican/Episcopal vestry books and parish registers** — many are at the Library of Virginia in original or microfilm form; the FamilySearch church records collection covers some but not all
- **Individual Baptist and Methodist congregation records** — scattered across local churches; not systematically digitized
- **Local DAR Genealogical Records Committee (GRC) reports** — compiled transcriptions at DAR headquarters and some state/local archives; not fully online

---

## Known record losses

- **1790 Virginia federal census: destroyed.** When British forces burned Washington in 1814, Virginia's 1790 census schedules were among the records lost. The 1782–1783 personal property tax lists (FamilySearch collection ID 1456723) are the primary substitute.
- **County courthouse fires:** Multiple Virginia courthouses burned over the centuries, destroying deed, probate, and court records for those counties. Notable losses include portions of records for several Tidewater and Piedmont counties. The Library of Virginia maintains a list of counties with known record losses — consult it before assuming records are extant for a specific county.
- **Anglican parish register losses:** The 1776 disestablishment of the Anglican Church left some registers without institutional custodians, leading to losses. Not all pre-1776 parishes have surviving registers.
- **Wartime disruption:** British raids on coastal Virginia communities (particularly 1779–1781) may have destroyed some local records, though the main courthouse records in the interior survived.

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with pensions before service records.** Revolutionary War pension files (NARA RG 15, on Fold3/Ancestry) are far more genealogically detailed than compiled service records. A pension narrative can include birthplace, parents' names, spouse's maiden name, children, and neighbors — all in the veteran's or widow's own words.

2. **Use the 1782 tax list as your census.** Since the 1790 Virginia census is destroyed, the 1782 personal property tax list (FamilySearch collection 1456723) is your earliest near-statewide enumeration. It lists all free white male tithables 16 and older, enslaved persons, and livestock — an effective substitute for a census head-of-household search.

3. **Identify the county at the event date, not today.** Virginia counties multiplied rapidly in this period. Use a historical county formation map (available at the Library of Virginia and in *Virginia County Formation* reference guides) to identify the correct county for a given location in a given year — and check the parent county for earlier records.

4. **Follow westward migrants back through Virginia.** Many families who appear in Kentucky, Tennessee, or Ohio records in the 1790s–1800s originated in Virginia counties in the 1770s–1780s. The Virginia Military District of Ohio bounty land records directly link Ohio settlers to their Virginia Revolutionary War service.

5. **Check both sides of the conflict.** If an ancestor is absent from patriot records, consider Loyalist records (British National Archives AO 12/AO 13) and oaths of allegiance filed in Virginia county courts. Both sets of records document wartime family activity.

6. **The FamilySearch Wiki article *Virginia Military Records*** (at [familysearch.org/en/wiki/Virginia_Military_Records](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Virginia_Military_Records)) is the authoritative starting point for navigating the scattered military record groups. Consult it before ordering records from NARA.

7. **County court order books are underused.** Beyond deeds and wills, Virginia county court order books record militia appointments, road overseer lists, orphan apprenticeships, and estate settlements — all of which identify wartime families and their neighbors.

8. **DAR and SAR applications point to already-located records.** If a lineage has already been proven for DAR/SAR membership, the application file cites primary sources found by the original applicant — saving duplication of effort.

---

## 📍 Locality Guide: New Orleans (Orleans Parish), Louisiana — Early 1800s (c. 1800–1850)

---

### Jurisdictional Context

New Orleans sits within **Orleans Parish**, Louisiana's equivalent of a county. The city was founded by France in 1718, transferred to Spain in 1763, briefly returned to France, and then sold to the United States in the **Louisiana Purchase (1803)**. During your target window it passed through three legal regimes:

| Period | Jurisdiction | Record Language |
|---|---|---|
| 1718–1769 | French colonial | French |
| 1769–1803 | Spanish colonial | Spanish |
| 1803–1812 | Territory of Orleans (U.S.) | English (& French) |
| 1812–1850 | State of Louisiana | English (& French) |

**Critically for researchers:** Louisiana retained its French/Spanish **civil law (Napoleonic Code)** tradition rather than adopting English common law. This means the record universe is structurally different from every other U.S. state — notarial records, not courthouse deed books, are the backbone of legal documentation, and probate is called "succession."

---

### Disasters and What Was Lost

Two catastrophic fires swept through New Orleans before the period under study:

- **Great Fire of 1788** — destroyed approximately 856 buildings, eliminating many colonial-era governmental and civil records.
- **Great Fire of 1794** — destroyed another ~212 buildings, including additional French Quarter structures.

Ongoing **yellow fever epidemics, hurricanes, and periodic flooding** throughout the 19th century further damaged archival holdings. The fires hit hardest at *civic and governmental* records of the Spanish colonial period. However, two categories of records largely survived because they were kept separately or in duplicate:

1. **Church sacramental registers** (kept at the cathedral, not in government buildings)
2. **Notarial acts** (kept by individual notaries, with some copies held in Spain)

---

### What Survived — Record by Record

---

#### 1. Church / Sacramental Records ⭐ *Primary vital records source*

**Why these survived:** St. Louis Cathedral kept its own registers, independent of the civil government buildings that burned. Catholic sacramental records are the *only* reliable source of births, marriages, and burials for this period — Louisiana did not have statewide civil registration of vital events until 1914.

**What the records contain:** Baptisms, marriages, and burials for all racial categories — white, free people of color (*gens de couleur libres*), and enslaved individuals. Records are in **French (pre-1769), Spanish (1769–1803), and English (after 1803)**; some bilingual entries exist in transitional years.

**Date coverage:** St. Louis Cathedral registers run from **1731 to the present**, with gaps in the colonial period due to fire damage.

**Where held:**
- **Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives** — original registers
- **FamilySearch (online):** [Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1612905) (Collection 1612905) — **indexed + images** available online; reported ~987,000 records and ~493,000 images as of the tool query

**Source:** FamilySearch Wiki, "Louisiana Church Records — Catholic Sacramental Records"

---

#### 2. Notarial Records ⭐ *Substitute for many destroyed civil records*

**Why these survived (partly):** Notaries kept their own protocol books, and Spanish colonial authorities maintained duplicate copies that were sent to Spain. The fires did destroy *some* notarial records, but the corpus is remarkably large.

**What the records contain:** This is Louisiana's richest record type for early researchers:
- **Successions** (estate inventories, wills — the Louisiana term for probate)
- **Marriage contracts** (pre-nuptial agreements, common in civil law)
- **Property transfers** (deeds, sales)
- **Tutorships** (guardianship of minors)
- **Emancipation documents** (freedom papers for enslaved individuals)
- **Slave transactions** (sales, hire agreements — name enslaved individuals)

**Organizing principle:** Notarial records are filed **by notary name and date**, not by the parties' names. You need an approximate date and the name of the notary used. This is the key research challenge.

**Where held:**
- **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** — holds over **30 million pages** of notarial acts from 1733 to the present
- **Louisiana Historical Center (Old U.S. Mint building)** — holds the colonial-era records: French period (1718–1769) and Spanish period (1769–1803)
- **Archivo General de Indias, Seville, Spain** — holds Spanish-era duplicate copies; useful when New Orleans originals are damaged

**FamilySearch digitization:** One browse-only volume found — **imageGroupNumber 004990115**, 167 images, covering **Notarial Acts (surviving microfilm copies), 1803–1820**, Orleans Parish. This volume is **0% name-indexed and not full-text searchable** — images must be browsed page by page. This is a significant coverage gap on FamilySearch; the main corpus remains physical at the Notarial Archives Research Center.

---

#### 3. Probate / Succession Records

Louisiana successions (probate) were filed at the **Orleans Parish Civil District Court** and are a major substitute source when other records are missing — estate inventories name heirs, list property, and frequently identify enslaved individuals.

**FamilySearch:** [Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2078341) (Collection 2078341) — **indexed + images**; reported ~456,000 records and ~228,000 images, date range comfortably spans the early 1800s.

**Louisiana State Archives (Baton Rouge)** also holds territorial and early statehood probate and court records.

---

#### 4. Census Records

| Year | Record | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1810 | Federal census, Territory of Orleans | Covers New Orleans; heads of household named |
| 1820 | Federal census, State of Louisiana | Heads of household named |
| 1830 | Federal census | Heads of household named |
| 1840 | Federal census | Heads of household named |

**Note on 1800:** The 1800 federal census for the Territory of Orleans has significant gaps and survival issues; coverage is incomplete for New Orleans specifically.

**Free people of color** were listed in a separate schedule category in all antebellum censuses. Enslaved individuals appear only as statistical counts under the slaveholder's name (1800–1840), or in a separate slave schedule (from 1850 onward).

**Access:** U.S. Federal Census records for this period are available on [Ancestry.com](https://www.ancestry.com/search/collections/census/) and FamilySearch.

---

#### 5. Land Records

**Spanish land grants** covering the colonial period are held at the **Louisiana State Land Office** and the **General Land Office (GLO)**. Post-1803 federal land patents are searchable at the [BLM-GLO website](https://glorecords.blm.gov/).

The **Louisiana Historical Center** holds Spanish colonial land survey records, another potential substitute when civil records were destroyed.

---

#### 6. Vital Records — Death Index (Post-Disaster Supplement)

**FamilySearch:** [Louisiana, Statewide Death Index, 1819–1964](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1543892) (Collection 1543892) — **index only, no images**; reported ~2.3 million entries. Useful for confirming deaths and obtaining original death record references, but the index alone does not provide full genealogical detail.

---

#### 7. Parish Marriages (Civil)

**FamilySearch:** [Louisiana, Parish Marriages, 1837–1957](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1798054) (Collection 1798054) — **indexed + images**; reported ~567,000 records and ~283,000 images. **Date caveat:** This collection starts in 1837, so it covers only the latter portion of the early 1800s target window. For marriages before 1837, rely on **church sacramental records** and **notarial marriage contracts**.

---

#### 8. Military Records

The **War of 1812** and the **Battle of New Orleans (January 1815)** generated significant service and pension records for Louisiana residents.

- **Service records and pension files:** [National Archives (NARA)](https://www.archives.gov/) — NARA's Southeast Region (Morrow, Georgia) holds regional federal records
- **Fold3:** [Fold3.com](https://www.fold3.com/) — digitized military records including War of 1812 service records

---

#### 9. Cemetery Records

**St. Louis Cemetery No. 1** (opened 1789) and **St. Louis Cemetery No. 2** (opened 1823) are the primary above-ground burial sites for New Orleans in this period. Cemetery transcriptions and photographs are available at [Find A Grave](https://www.findagrave.com/). Cemetery burials can serve as substitute death records when church burial registers have gaps.

---

#### 10. Newspapers (Substitute Source)

New Orleans had an active press in both French and English by the early 1800s. Notices of births, deaths, marriages, and estate sales were commonly published. Historical newspapers are available at [Newspapers.com](https://www.newspapers.com/) and through the **Louisiana Digital Library**.

---

### Special Populations — Key Guidance

**Free People of Color (*Gens de Couleur Libres*):** New Orleans had one of the largest free Black communities in antebellum America. Records appear in all the above categories — church baptisms (often noting godparents and racial status), notarial emancipation documents, succession records, and census free-population schedules.

**Enslaved Individuals:** Names and ages appear in:
- Church baptismal registers (slaveholder often listed as godparent or listed separately)
- Notarial acts: emancipation documents, estate inventories, sale contracts
- Slave manifests for coastal trade (held at NARA)
- Slaveholder's census entry (1800–1840) or the 1850/1860 slave schedules

---

### Disaster Substitution Strategy — Summary

| Lost or Damaged Record | Best Substitute(s) |
|---|---|
| Pre-1800 civil vital records (fires) | Church sacramental registers (St. Louis Cathedral) |
| Marriage records before 1837 | Notarial marriage contracts; church registers |
| Pre-statehood probate | Notarial succession acts; Spanish colonial records at Louisiana Historical Center |
| Colonial land/property records | Spanish grants at Louisiana State Land Office; GLO |
| Death records before 1819 | Church burial registers; cemetery records; newspaper notices |
| Records with no English equivalent | Spanish-period duplicates at Archivo General de Indias (Seville) |

---

### Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** | 30M+ pages of notarial acts, 1733–present | In-person; some microfilm via FamilySearch |
| **Louisiana Historical Center (Old U.S. Mint)** | French & Spanish colonial records, 1718–1803 | In-person |
| **Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives** | St. Louis Cathedral sacramental registers, 1731–present | In-person; microfilm/online via FamilySearch |
| **Louisiana State Archives, Baton Rouge** | Territorial & state records, probate, court files | In-person |
| **Orleans Parish Civil District Court** | Successions, civil suits, tutorships | In-person |
| **NARA Southeast Region (Morrow, GA)** | Federal territorial records, military, naturalization | In-person; some online via Fold3/Ancestry |
| **FamilySearch (online)** | Church records 1718–1925; probate 1756–1984; death index 1819–1964; notarial browse-only vol. 1803–1820 | Free online |
| **Ancestry.com** | Federal census; military records | Subscription |

---

### Coverage Gaps to Note

- The **FamilySearch notarial volume** (1803–1820) contains only 167 browse-only images — a narrow slice of the 30-million-page corpus at the Notarial Archives Research Center. The vast majority of notarial records require an in-person or correspondence visit.
- **No civil registration** of births, marriages, or deaths existed in Louisiana during this period (statewide registration began 1914). Church records are the substitute, not a supplement.
- The **1800 federal census** for New Orleans has incomplete survival.
- FamilySearch's curated external-links tool returned only general-purpose resources (Ancestry census, Find A Grave, Fold3, Newspapers.com, NARA) — no New Orleans-specific digitization projects were surfaced, suggesting specialized local databases (e.g., the Notarial Archives' own online index) are outside FamilySearch's curated list and must be sought directly.

---

### Suggested Next Step

If you'd like to move from this survey to a sequenced research plan for a specific ancestor or question, the **research-plan** skill can build one using these record sets in priority order. Just let me know the research question (e.g., "Who were the parents of Jean-Baptiste Moreau, born New Orleans c. 1815?") and I'll build the plan.
