Here it is:

---

# Locality Guide: Oklahoma Territory & Indian Territory (1889–1907)
### *For Families Connected to the Oklahoma Land Run of 1889*

---

## Jurisdiction Overview

- **Event date:** April 22, 1889 — President Benjamin Harrison opened approximately two million acres of the Unassigned Lands at noon; an estimated 50,000 participants raced to stake claims *(Wikipedia: "Land Run of 1889")*
- **Formal establishment:** Oklahoma Territory created by the Organic Act of May 2, 1890, with Guthrie as the capital
- **Statehood:** November 16, 1907 — Oklahoma Territory and Indian Territory merged to form the state of Oklahoma
- **Economy:** Subsistence farming, homesteading, cattle ranching; new town commerce (Guthrie, Kingfisher, Oklahoma City)
- **Settlement origins:** Many Land Run settlers arrived from Kansas, Texas, Missouri, and other neighboring states *(FamilySearch Wiki: Oklahoma Territorial Records)*
- **Population data:** No FamilySearch fixture data was returned for this period; use the 1890 Oklahoma Territory census and the 1900/1910 federal censuses as primary enumerations
- **Religious denominations:** Baptist, Methodist, and Catholic congregations established from 1889 onward *(FamilySearch Wiki: Oklahoma Vital Records)*

---

## ⚠️ Critical Dual-Jurisdiction Warning

**This is the single most important research fact for this period.** Between 1889 and 1907, two entirely separate jurisdictions operated side by side, each generating different record sets:

| Jurisdiction | Geography | Governing Authority | Key Records |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Oklahoma Territory** | Western/central Oklahoma (Land Run counties) | Federal/Territorial government | Homestead files, territorial courts, county records |
| **Indian Territory** | Eastern Oklahoma | Five Civilized Tribes (Cherokee, Choctaw, Chickasaw, Creek, Seminole) | Dawes Rolls, tribal records, missionary church records |

Before searching, determine which jurisdiction your ancestor lived in. Records created in Indian Territory follow a completely different trail than those in Oklahoma Territory.

---

## Boundary Changes

- **Before April 22, 1889:** The Unassigned Lands were unorganized federal domain — no civil infrastructure, no county record-keeping
- **May 2, 1890:** Oklahoma Territory formally established; counties began organizing and creating official records
- **1889–1907:** The two-territory split meant a family in Guthrie (Oklahoma Territory) and one in Tahlequah (Indian Territory, Cherokee Nation) required completely different research strategies
- **Subsequent land runs** (1891, 1892, 1893, 1895) opened additional tracts, progressively extending Oklahoma Territory eastward *(Wikipedia)*
- **1907:** Both territories merged at statehood; county records from both territories transferred to successor county courthouses and the Oklahoma State Archives

*Records created before a county was organized (i.e., before 1890) were typically federal in nature — there was no county courthouse to hold them.*

---

## Available Record Types

### Land Records ⭐ *Start Here*

Land records are the most important source for 1889–1907 Oklahoma Territory research. The homestead process generated a paper trail at every step.

- **What exists:** Homestead applications, proof-of-settlement statements (with named witnesses), final patents, and rejected applications. Each file may include the claimant's age, origin state, physical description of improvements, and names of neighbors serving as witnesses *(FamilySearch Wiki: Oklahoma Land Records)*
- **Original filing offices:** Guthrie and Kingfisher land offices *(Wikipedia)*
- **Where held — NARA:** Original case files in Record Group 49, General Land Office, National Archives, Washington, D.C. Partially also at the National Archives at Fort Worth
- **Where held — BLM GLO:** **[glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov)** — free searchable database of Oklahoma land patents, many with downloadable images
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907* — 234,567 records, 117,283 images — **indexed + images** — [Collection 1541872](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1541872)
- **Tip:** Even rejected applications survive and may contain family details. Proof-of-settlement documents name witnesses who were often neighbors or relatives.

---

### Indian Censuses and Tribal Rolls

Essential for any family in Indian Territory, and also relevant for Oklahoma Territory families with Native American ancestry or neighbors.

- **What exists:** Tribal census rolls and citizenship records spanning 1851–1959; the Dawes Commission rolls (1898–1914) are the primary enrollment of the Five Civilized Tribes documenting tribal citizenship
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959* — 567,890 records, 283,945 images — **indexed + images** — [Collection 1987432](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1987432)
- **Access:** Also searchable through Ancestry.com; originals at NARA

---

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)

- **Start date:** Statewide vital registration began **October 1908**; compliance remained poor until the 1930s *(FamilySearch Wiki: Oklahoma Vital Records)*
- **For 1889–1908:** No civil registration existed. Births, marriages, and deaths during the territorial period must be documented through substitute sources (church records, cemetery records, territorial court records, newspapers)
- **What exists post-1908:** Oklahoma State Archives holds birth and death records from 1908 onward
- **Marriage exception:** County marriage records begin in 1890, when counties were organized — see County Marriages below

---

### County Marriage Records

- **FamilySearch collection:** *Oklahoma, County Marriages, 1890–1995* — 456,789 records, 913,578 persons indexed, 228,394 images — **indexed + images** — [Collection 2134789](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2134789)
- **Date range:** Begins 1890, when county governments were established following the Organic Act
- **Where held (originals):** County courthouse in the county where the marriage was recorded
- **Note:** Marriage records are often the earliest vital-type record available for territorial settlers

---

### Church Records

- **Denominations:** Baptist, Methodist, and Catholic congregations began establishing churches from 1889 onward in the new settlements; missionary churches served Native communities in Indian Territory from earlier decades
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records, 1820–1965* — 123,456 records, 61,728 images — **indexed + images** — [Collection 1654321](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1654321)
- **Physical repositories:**
  - Oklahoma Conference of the United Methodist Church Archives *(FamilySearch Wiki: Oklahoma Vital Records)*
  - Diocese of Oklahoma City (Catholic parish registers) *(FamilySearch Wiki: Oklahoma Vital Records)*
  - Local congregation offices for Baptist and other denominations
- **Research value:** The primary substitute for birth, baptism, and burial records during the 1889–1908 gap before civil registration

---

### Census Records

- **1890 Oklahoma Territory Census** ⭐ — One of the very few surviving 1890 census records in the nation (the regular 1890 federal census was mostly destroyed by fire in 1921). Held by the Oklahoma Historical Society; also available through NARA and FamilySearch. This is an exceptional survival and should be searched early *(FamilySearch Wiki: Oklahoma Land Records)*
- **1900 Federal Census:** Both Oklahoma Territory and Indian Territory were enumerated separately; available through NARA, FamilySearch, and Ancestry.com — **indexed + images**
- **1910 Federal Census:** First census after statehood; fully indexed — **indexed + images**
- **County tax rolls (1890s):** County-level tax rolls from the 1890s survive and can help document early settlers in the years between the 1890 territorial census and the 1900 federal census *(FamilySearch Wiki: Oklahoma Territorial Records)*

---

### Probate and Court Records

- **Territorial court records (1890–1907):** District court civil and criminal cases, probate, and naturalization records for Oklahoma Territory
- **Where held:** National Archives at Fort Worth (NARA Southwest Region) and the Oklahoma State Archives *(FamilySearch Wiki: Oklahoma Territorial Records)*
- **Indian Territory courts:** Separate federal courts (U.S. Courts for the Indian Territory) operated in eastern Oklahoma; records also at NARA Fort Worth
- **Digitization status:** Largely **physical/microfilm** — plan to contact NARA Fort Worth or the Oklahoma State Archives for access
- **Research value:** Probate records from even the earliest county organization (1890+) name heirs and family members; naturalization files document immigrant settlers' origins

---

### Military Records

- **Relevant conflicts for Land Run settlers:** Civil War veterans (many were of service age in 1861–65 and settler age in 1889); Spanish-American War (1898); Indian Wars service in Oklahoma Territory
- **Where held:** Service records and pension files at NARA Washington, D.C.; **Fold3** (subscription) offers digitized military records *(external links tool)*
- **Research value:** Civil War pension files are among the most genealogically rich federal records, often including birth information, marriage records, and widow's statements

---

### Cemetery Records

- **Online access:** **Find A Grave** ([findagrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com)) — free; indexed by volunteer contributors *(external links tool)*
- **Coverage note:** Early settlement cemeteries (1889–1900) may have sparse or no online coverage; many family and rural cemeteries were established before formal cemetery records were kept
- **Physical records:** Sexton records (when they exist) are held by individual cemeteries or local historical societies
- **Tip:** The Oklahoma Cemetery Registry, maintained by the Oklahoma Historical Society, is a useful finding aid for locating rural and family cemeteries

---

### Newspaper Records

- **Online access:** **Newspapers.com** (subscription) *(external links tool)*; also check the Oklahoma Historical Society's digital newspaper archive
- **Territorial papers:** Newspapers were established in the new settlements almost immediately after the 1889 Land Run — Guthrie and Oklahoma City had papers running within days of the opening
- **Research value:** Land claim notices, legal notices (required by territorial law), marriage announcements, obituaries, community news, and Sooner/settler disputes that appeared in court columns

---

### Tax Records

- **What exists:** County-level tax rolls from the 1890s *(FamilySearch Wiki: Oklahoma Territorial Records)*
- **Where held:** Oklahoma Historical Society and county courthouses — largely **physical only** or microfilm
- **Substitute value:** Tax rolls from 1891–1899 can place a settler in a specific county in years between the 1890 territorial census and the 1900 federal census

---

### Immigration and Naturalization Records

- **Context:** Many Land Run settlers were recent immigrants or first-generation Americans; naturalization was a prerequisite to filing a homestead claim under territorial law
- **Pre-1906 naturalizations:** Filed in any court of record — territorial district courts, county courts. Records scattered across NARA Fort Worth and county courthouses
- **Post-1906:** Filed through federal courts; NARA holds these records
- **Where held:** NARA (passenger lists); NARA Fort Worth and Oklahoma State Archives (territorial naturalizations)

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | Collections for this place | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | *Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907* (234,567 records); *Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959* (567,890 records); *Oklahoma, County Marriages, 1890–1995* (456,789 records); *Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records, 1820–1965* (123,456 records) | Free |
| **BLM General Land Office** ([glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov)) | Oklahoma land patents with images | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census Collection (1900, 1910); Dawes Rolls; other Oklahoma collections | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Military service records, pension files | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical Oklahoma territorial newspapers | Subscription |
| **Find A Grave** | Cemetery records and headstone photographs | Free |
| **NARA (archives.gov)** | Federal records, homestead files (some online via Ancestry/FamilySearch) | Free to search; copies may have fees |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Oklahoma Historical Society** (Oklahoma City) | 1890 Oklahoma Territory census; land run claim files; town lot records; early county records; newspaper archives; cemetery registry | In-person; some materials digitized |
| **Oklahoma State Archives** (Oklahoma City) | Birth and death records from 1908; state government records; territorial records | In-person; mail requests |
| **National Archives at Fort Worth** (NARA Southwest Region) | Territorial court records 1890–1907; Indian Territory court records; federal agency records | In-person; duplication requests |
| **NARA Washington, D.C.** | Record Group 49 (General Land Office homestead files); military service and pension records; passenger lists | In-person; paid duplication; many records on Ancestry/FamilySearch |
| **County Courthouses** | Deed books, probate files, court records, marriage records (originals) from county organization (1890+) | In-person / mail |
| **Oklahoma Conference, United Methodist Church Archives** | Methodist congregation registers from 1889 onward | Contact archives for access |
| **Diocese of Oklahoma City** | Catholic parish registers | Contact diocesan archives |

---

## Records NOT Online

The following records exist but are not fully available in any online database — do not assume they don't exist because they don't appear in a web search:

- **Original homestead case files** (application papers, proof-of-settlement witness statements): Only the patents are widely searchable online; the full case files are at NARA Washington, D.C., and must be ordered
- **Territorial court records (probate, naturalization, civil cases, 1890–1907):** Largely held physically at NARA Fort Worth and the Oklahoma State Archives
- **County tax rolls (1890s):** Primarily physical at the Oklahoma Historical Society and county courthouses
- **Most pre-1908 church registers:** Not comprehensively digitized; contact individual denomination archives

---

## Known Record Losses

- **1890 Federal Census:** The regular federal census for Oklahoma (and nearly all U.S. states) was largely destroyed by fire in Washington, D.C., in 1921. The **1890 Oklahoma Territory special census** is a notable and valuable survivor — search it specifically
- **Early territorial period (before May 1890):** No county governments existed before the Organic Act, meaning there was no county-level record infrastructure for the period between the April 22, 1889, Land Run and the formal establishment of counties

---

## Research Tips

1. **Start with the homestead file.** For any Land Run settler, the homestead application at NARA (and partially on FamilySearch and BLM GLO) is the richest early record — it names the claimant, origin state, describes improvements, and includes sworn witness statements that often name neighbors and family.

2. **Determine which territory first.** Before searching, map your ancestor's land to either Oklahoma Territory (western/central) or Indian Territory (eastern). The record sets are almost entirely different. County atlases and the BLM GLO plat maps help with this.

3. **Search the 1890 Oklahoma Territory census immediately.** It's one of the only surviving 1890 enumerations in the country and predates any state or county civil registration.

4. **No civil registration until 1908.** For births, deaths, and all vital events before statehood, pivot to church records, cemetery records, territorial court files (especially guardianship and probate records, which name children and heirs), and newspapers.

5. **Trace origins in departure states.** Many 1889 settlers came from Kansas, Texas, and Missouri *(FamilySearch Wiki: Oklahoma Territorial Records)*. Finding the family in Kansas, Missouri, or Texas records before 1889 often unlocks parentage and earlier generations.

6. **If there's any Native American connection, the Dawes Rolls are essential.** The FamilySearch collection *Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959* and Ancestry's Dawes Roll databases document Five Civilized Tribes citizenship applications — including applications that were rejected, which still name individuals and families.

7. **Use county marriage records as the earliest vital-type source.** County Marriages begin in 1890; combined with the Mission and Church Records collection, these are the primary tools for vital event documentation before 1908.

8. **NARA Fort Worth is your territorial court repository.** For probate, naturalization, and court records from Oklahoma Territory and Indian Territory (1890–1907), contact the National Archives at Fort Worth (Southwest Region) — this is not well-represented in online databases.

---

# Locality Guide: Alabama Region (circa 1780–1820, Pre-Statehood)

## Jurisdiction overview

- **Primary jurisdiction during target period:** Mississippi Territory (1798–1817), then Alabama Territory (1817–1819); Alabama achieved statehood December 14, 1819
- **Administrative seat:** Washington, Mississippi Territory (later Huntsville, Alabama Territory)
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Mississippi Territory was carved from land ceded to the U.S. after the Revolution; the Alabama strip was added in 1804
- **Earlier sovereignty:** French (1702–1763), British (1763–1780), Spanish (1780–1813) over the Mobile District; American/territorial authority was sparse and contested until the Creek War of 1813–1814
- **Population:** The federal census did not reach the Alabama portion until 1810 (as part of Mississippi Territory). Territorial censuses (below) supply the earlier population snapshot. Significant in-migration from the Carolinas, Virginia, and Georgia began accelerating after 1814.
- **Economy:** Subsistence agriculture, fur trade, early cotton cultivation; the Mobile Bay area supported maritime trade
- **Ethnic composition:** Anglo-American settlers from the southeastern seaboard; French Creole and Spanish Creole families (especially Mobile); Creek and Cherokee nations controlling large interior portions; small free Black and enslaved African-descended populations
- **Religious denominations:** Roman Catholic (Mobile, with Spanish colonial roots); Baptist and Methodist circuit riders among Anglo settlers; Protestant church organization was informal and sparse before 1810
- **Key historical events:** Creek War of 1813–1814 (opened interior lands to Anglo settlement); War of 1812 (Battle of Mobile); U.S. seizure of Mobile from Spain in 1813

---

## Boundary changes

This is the most consequential factor for research in this period. **Records follow the jurisdiction that created them, not modern Alabama county lines.**

- **Before 1798:** No American territorial government. Records created by Spanish colonial authorities (Mobile District), or are absent for the interior.
- **1798:** Mississippi Territory established. The Alabama-region counties were organized within it — Washington County (1800) and Baldwin County (1809) were the first in present-day Alabama.
- **1804:** The northern strip (Tombigbee valley) was added to the Territory; Madison County organized 1808.
- **1817:** Alabama Territory separated from Mississippi Territory. At this point, records for the Alabama region began accumulating under an Alabama-specific government.
- **1819:** Statehood. County-level record-keeping accelerates.

**Research implication:** Records created before 1817 for most of present-day Alabama are technically *Mississippi Territory* records, not Alabama records. Land records for the territorial period may be held or catalogued under Mississippi. The counties that existed across the dividing line (1817) hold the continuity of record creation.

---

## Available record types

### Vital records (civil registration)

- **Start date:** Alabama did not enact statewide civil registration of births and deaths until 1908. **There is no civil vital registration for this period.**
- **Marriages:** County clerks began issuing marriage bonds and licenses as counties were organized, beginning approximately 1809. FamilySearch holds **"Alabama, County Marriages, 1809–1950"** (collection #1598408) — 612,345 indexed records with 306,172 images. Digitization level: **indexed + images**. The 1809 start date makes this the earliest indexed civil record class available online for the Alabama region.
- **Births/deaths:** No civil registration. Substitute sources are church records, Bible records, and obituary notices.

### Church records

- **Catholic (Mobile area):** The Roman Catholic parish at Mobile was established under French colonial rule in the early 1700s. Spanish colonial church registers (baptisms, marriages, burials) for the Mobile District are among the oldest continuous records for the region.
  - Original registers: Archivo General de Indias, Seville, Spain
  - Copies: Library of Congress (per the FamilySearch wiki "Alabama Genealogy" article, Colonial Records section)
  - Microfilm: Mobile Municipal Archives holds microfilm of the French-period colonial records
- **Protestant settlers:** Baptist and Methodist congregations formed in the first decade of the 1800s. Records are fragmentary and scattered across individual church archives, denominational repositories, and local historical societies. Formal record-keeping was inconsistent in this frontier period.
- **FamilySearch:** **"Alabama, Church Records, 1803–1950"** (collection #1986396) — 156,789 indexed records with 78,394 images. Digitization level: **indexed + images**. Earliest records begin 1803.

### Census records

The 1800 federal census does **not** cover the Alabama region — the area had no organized American territory at that time. The Mississippi Territory was enumerated separately.

- **Mississippi Territory territorial censuses:** Censuses of 1801, 1805, 1808, and 1810 enumerated settlers in present-day Alabama. These are held at the **Alabama Department of Archives and History (ADAH)** in Montgomery (per FamilySearch wiki "Alabama Genealogy," Territorial Records section). Physical/microfilm access; availability online should be verified directly with ADAH.
- **1810 federal census:** The first federal census to include the Alabama portion of Mississippi Territory. Covers what would become Alabama.
- **1820 federal census:** First census under Alabama statehood.
- **FamilySearch:** **"Alabama, Compiled Census and Census Substitutes Index, 1810–1890"** (collection #2016756) — 287,634 indexed records, **no images**. Digitization level: **index only** (no images linked). This is an index to multiple census and substitute sources; the underlying images must be located separately.
- **Mississippi-scope:** **"Mississippi, Compiled Census and Census Substitutes, 1792–1890"** (collection #1927171, scoped to Mississippi) — 198,340 records, **no images**. The 1792–1817 portion covers the territorial period and may include Alabama-area enumerations.

### Probate and court records

- **Probate:** County-level, organized as counties formed. FamilySearch holds **"Alabama, Probate Records, 1809–1985"** (collection #1537764) — 438,210 indexed records with 219,105 images. Digitization level: **indexed + images**. The 1809 start reflects when the first Alabama-area counties had organized probate courts.
- **Territorial/Mississippi court records:** FamilySearch holds **"Mississippi, County Court Records, 1799–1920"** (collection #1855293) — 245,670 records with 122,835 images. This collection covers the Mississippi Territory period (1799–1817) when Alabama-area counties fell under Mississippi jurisdiction. Digitization level: **indexed + images**.
- **Types:** Wills, estate inventories, guardianship appointments, letters of administration, civil suits. Probate records in this period often name all heirs, making them a primary substitute for missing vital records.

### Land records

Land records are the **single richest surviving record class** for the pre-statehood Alabama region, and several categories are online.

- **Federal land state:** Alabama is a federal-land state (not a state-land state). Original land patents were issued by the U.S. General Land Office. The **BLM General Land Office Records website** (glorecords.blm.gov) provides free, searchable access to federal land patents. The earliest Alabama patents date to the territorial period (post-1805 land cessions). Digitization level: **indexed + images** (patent documents are scanned and freely searchable online).
- **Territorial land records:** FamilySearch holds **"Mississippi, Territorial and State Land Records, 1798–1907"** (collection #1919694) — 312,450 records with 156,225 images. This covers the territorial period when Alabama fell under Mississippi Territory jurisdiction. Digitization level: **indexed + images**.
- **Spanish and British colonial grants (Mobile area):** The earliest land documentation for the Mobile District consists of Spanish colonial grants (1780–1813) and earlier British grants (1763–1780).
  - Spanish grants: Archivo General de Indias, Seville; copies at the Library of Congress
  - These grants underlie many early American-era title claims and appear in later territorial court proceedings
- **Tax lists:** Territorial-era tax lists survive for some counties and serve as census substitutes. Held at ADAH; some transcribed by genealogical societies.

### Military records

- **Creek War (1813–1814):** Militia and volunteer rosters from this conflict are among the most genealogically useful military records for this period. Records are at the National Archives (NARA, RG 94) and the Alabama Department of Archives and History. Fold3.com (subscription) hosts many NARA military records.
- **War of 1812:** Mobile area operations generated service and pension records. NARA holds service records; pension files are at NARA and searchable on Fold3 and Ancestry.
- **Territorial militia rolls:** Listed among the record types in the FamilySearch wiki "Alabama Genealogy" (Territorial Records section). Held at ADAH; digitization status should be confirmed directly.
- **Spanish colonial militia:** Records for the Spanish-period Mobile District militia are at the Archivo General de Indias.

### Cemetery records

- **Online coverage:** FindAGrave (findagrave.com) is the primary free database. Coverage for this early period is sparse — headstones from 1800–1820 that survive are often plain field stones without inscriptions.
- **Early cemeteries:** Family plots on homestead land were common; many were never formally recorded. Plantation burial grounds existed but records are largely lost.
- **Physical records:** No systematic sexton records from this period; church burial registers (Catholic Mobile) are the closest equivalent.

### Newspaper records

- **Early papers:** Newspapers in the Alabama region are rare before statehood. The *Mobile Centinel* (est. ~1811) is among the earliest. The *Huntsville Republican* began around 1816.
- **Access:** The Library of Congress *Chronicling America* project (chroniclingamerica.loc.gov) provides free, full-text searchable access to early Alabama newspapers. Coverage for pre-1820 papers is limited.
- **Value:** Notices of land sales, estate settlements, and migration announcements in newspapers can supplement missing records.

### Land entry case files

- A specialized but important source: when settlers filed for federal land, the General Land Office created case files that can include sworn testimony, neighbor names, and family detail beyond what the patent itself shows. NARA holds these (RG 49); some are microfilmed.

### Tax records

- Territorial-era tax lists function as annual census substitutes — they enumerate adult (usually male) property owners and can place a person in a specific county in specific years between censuses.
- Per the FamilySearch wiki ("Alabama Genealogy," Territorial Records), tax lists are among the records of the territorial period held at ADAH.
- Digitization level: **physical/microfilm** — confirm with ADAH whether specific lists have been digitized.

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Collections relevant to this period | Access |
|---|---|---|
| FamilySearch (familysearch.org) | Alabama County Marriages 1809–1950 (#1598408); Alabama Probate Records 1809–1985 (#1537764); Alabama Compiled Census & Substitutes Index 1810–1890 (#2016756); Alabama Church Records 1803–1950 (#1986396); Mississippi Territorial & State Land Records 1798–1907 (#1919694); Mississippi County Court Records 1799–1920 (#1855293) | Free |
| BLM General Land Office Records (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents for Alabama, searchable from territorial period onward | Free |
| Chronicling America — Library of Congress (chroniclingamerica.loc.gov) | Early Alabama newspapers (sparse pre-1820) | Free |
| Ancestry (ancestry.com) | U.S. Federal Census; some overlapping Alabama and territorial collections | Subscription |
| Fold3 (fold3.com) | Creek War and War of 1812 military records; NARA pension files | Subscription |
| Library of Congress (loc.gov) | Copies of Spanish colonial records for Mobile District | Free (finding aids online; digital availability varies) |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| Alabama Department of Archives and History (ADAH), Montgomery, AL | Mississippi Territory censuses 1801/1805/1808/1810; territorial tax lists; territorial militia rolls; early county records; state records post-1819 | In-person; some online via ADAH website |
| National Archives (NARA), Atlanta (SE Region) | Federal court records; some territorial records | In-person; mail/FOIA requests |
| NARA, Washington DC / St. Louis | Military service and pension records (Creek War, War of 1812); federal land entry case files (RG 49) | In-person; online via Ancestry/Fold3 |
| Mobile Municipal Archives, Mobile, AL | Microfilm of French colonial records (Mobile area); local government records | In-person |
| Archivo General de Indias, Seville, Spain | Spanish colonial records for the Mobile District (censuses, church registers, land grants, militia) | In-person; some microfilm via Library of Congress |
| Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer, Aix-en-Provence, France | French colonial records (Mobile, pre-1763) | In-person; some microfilm at Mobile Municipal Archives |
| County courthouses (Washington, Baldwin, Madison, and other early counties) | Original deed books, marriage bonds, probate files from county formation forward | In-person; some records microfilmed by FamilySearch |

---

## Digitized, browsable volumes — direct answer to your question

**FamilySearch volume search returned zero results** for Alabama in the 1780–1820 window, and also zero results for Mississippi Territory in 1798–1820. This means FamilySearch's Records Management Service does not surface any named browse-only image volumes specifically catalogued to this jurisdiction and period — this is a real coverage gap, not an oversight.

What **is** available online with images comes through the **indexed collections** listed above (which have images attached to indexed records, not as standalone browsable volumes):
- Alabama County Marriages 1809–1950: 306,172 images, indexed
- Alabama Probate Records 1809–1985: 219,105 images, indexed
- Alabama Church Records 1803–1950: 78,394 images, indexed
- Mississippi Territorial & State Land Records 1798–1907: 156,225 images, indexed
- Mississippi County Court Records 1799–1920: 122,835 images, indexed

For genuinely **browse-only (unindexed) digitized images**, the most promising avenue is the **BLM GLO** (fully browsable land patents) and **Chronicling America** (full-text searchable newspapers). ADAH has been digitizing some holdings; their online catalog at archives.alabama.gov is worth checking directly for any territorial-era items posted since the FamilySearch corpus was last updated.

---

## Records NOT online

- Mississippi Territory censuses (1801, 1805, 1808, 1810) — held at ADAH; not currently in any online indexed database per FamilySearch wiki
- Territorial militia rolls — held at ADAH; not online
- Territorial-era tax lists — held at ADAH; not consistently online
- Spanish colonial records (Mobile District) — original at Archivo General de Indias; some microfilm at Library of Congress, not broadly digitized online
- French colonial records (pre-1763 Mobile) — original in France; microfilm at Mobile Municipal Archives
- Federal land entry case files (RG 49, NARA) — not broadly digitized; require NARA request
- Early county deed books (originals) — at county courthouses; some microfilmed by FamilySearch but not surfaced in the volume search for this period

---

## Known record losses

- **Interior Alabama (pre-1810):** For the vast majority of present-day Alabama outside the Mobile Bay area, essentially no government records were created before county organization (ca. 1800–1810). This is a structural gap, not a destruction — record-keeping infrastructure simply did not exist.
- **Creek Nation records:** The Creek and Cherokee nations controlled much of the interior. Their internal records from this period are largely not accessible through standard genealogical repositories.
- **Early courthouse losses:** Some early county records were lost to courthouse fires in the nineteenth century. Verify record survival at ADAH for specific counties before assuming completeness.

---

## Research tips

1. **Think in jurisdictions, not modern state lines.** Any record created in present-day Alabama before 1817 is a Mississippi Territory record. Search both Alabama and Mississippi collections, and look for case files and land records filed under Mississippi jurisdiction.

2. **Land records first.** For this period, land is the richest and best-preserved record class. Start with BLM GLO for federal patents and the Mississippi Territorial Land Records collection (#1919694) on FamilySearch, then trace backward through Spanish/British colonial grants if your family was in the Mobile area.

3. **Use probate records as vital-record substitutes.** With no civil birth/death registration, estate inventories and will books (beginning ~1809) are the primary sources for family relationships. They name spouses, children, and sometimes extended kin.

4. **Contact ADAH directly.** The Alabama Department of Archives and History is the custodian of territorial-era records not yet online. Their staff can confirm whether specific territorial censuses, tax lists, or militia rolls for a county and year have been microfilmed or digitized.

5. **Mobile-area researchers: pursue Spanish records.** If your family was in the Mobile Bay region before 1813, the Spanish colonial church and land records are substantial and genealogically detailed — Catholic baptism registers in particular. The Library of Congress holds copies; contact the Archives and Special Collections at the University of South Alabama for guidance on accessing Mobile-area colonial records.

6. **The Creek War of 1813–1814 is a migration trigger.** Many Anglo families moved into Alabama *after* this war. If you can't find a family before ~1815, search for them in the Carolinas, Virginia, Tennessee, or Georgia — that's where the pre-war trail typically leads.

7. **FamilySearch wiki article:** "Alabama Genealogy" (https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Alabama_Genealogy) — particularly the Territorial Records and Colonial Records sections — is the key starting reference for this period.
